prompt: A pass goes out-of-bounds and the opposing team gains possession. The new thrower carries the disc to the correct spot on the central zone and establishes a pivot. What must the thrower do next to legally continue play?
choices: Touch the disc to the ground before putting it into play.
Offer the disc to the nearest defensive player for a check.
Wait for the defense to set up before making a pass.
Announce “Disc in” loudly before throwing.
explanation: According to 11.H, after establishing a pivot at the appropriate spot, the thrower must touch the disc to the ground before putting it into play.

prompt: A pass is thrown near the sideline. Which of the following events causes the disc to immediately become out-of-bounds?
choices: It is caught by an out-of-bounds defensive player.
It is deflected by an in-bounds defensive player and flies outside the perimeter line.
It passes completely outside the perimeter line in the air.
It is contacted by an in-bounds offensive player whose momentum is carrying them out-of-bounds.
explanation: Under rule 11.E, a disc becomes out-of-bounds when it first contacts the out-of-bounds area, contacts an out-of-bounds offensive player, or is caught by an out-of-bounds defensive player.

prompt: A receiver catches a pass near the edge of the field. Their first point of contact with the ground is directly on the solid perimeter line. What is the status of the player?
choices: They are out-of-bounds, because the perimeter lines are not part of the playing field.
They are in-bounds, because the perimeter lines are part of the playing field.
They are in-bounds as long as no part of their foot touches the ground outside the line.
They are out-of-bounds only if they straddle the line, touching the outside area at the same time.
explanation: Rule 11.A states that the entire playing field is in-bounds, and the perimeter lines are not part of the playing field and are out-of-bounds.

prompt: An errant pass hits an observer who is standing out-of-bounds, then bounces back into the playing field where it is caught by an offensive player. What happens to the status of the disc?
choices: It does not change status; the disc is still live and the catch stands.
It becomes out-of-bounds immediately upon contacting the observer.
It becomes a turnover and is put into play where the observer was standing.
It is returned to the thrower because observers are considered part of the out-of-bounds area.
explanation: Rule 11.B states that a disc does not change in- or out-of-bounds status when contacted by an observer. Therefore, it remains live.

prompt: A player starts entirely out-of-bounds, jumps into the air, catches the disc while airborne, and lands completely in-bounds. What is the status of the player when they catch the disc?
choices: The player is out-of-bounds because they retain their out-of-bounds status until contacting the playing field.
The player is in-bounds because they caught the disc over the in-bounds playing field.
The player is in-bounds because their first point of ground contact after catching the disc is in-bounds.
The player is out-of-bounds, but only if the disc was caught outside the perimeter line.
explanation: According to 11.C, an airborne player retains their in-bounds or out-of-bounds status until they contact the playing field or the out-of-bounds area. Since they started out-of-bounds, they remain out-of-bounds while airborne.

prompt: An offensive player catches an in-bounds disc and would easily have landed in-bounds. However, they safely land on a defender's foot, causing the offensive player to stumble and make their first ground contact out-of-bounds. How is this resolved?
choices: It is treated as a force-out foul, assuming the play resulting in contact cannot be construed as a dangerous play.
It is a turnover because the offensive player's first ground contact was out-of-bounds.
The disc is returned to the thrower because the contact was incidental.
It is a dangerous play foul on the defender for undercutting the receiver.
explanation: Under 11.C.4, if a player catches an in-bounds disc and would have landed in-bounds but lands on an opposing player causing their first contact to be out-of-bounds, it is treated as a force-out foul, provided the play is not a dangerous play.

prompt: A disc has been carried out-of-bounds. According to the rules, when does the disc legally become “in-bounds” again?
choices: When it is put into play, or when play starts or restarts.
When the thrower establishes a pivot foot on the central zone.
As soon as it crosses the perimeter line in the air.
When the thrower releases the disc for a pass.
explanation: Rule 11.D explicitly states that a disc becomes in-bounds when it is put into play, or when play starts or restarts.

prompt: An offensive player sees a pass curving outside the sideline. Can the player run outside the perimeter line to attempt a play on the disc?
choices: Yes, players may go out-of-bounds to make a play on the disc, and the disc may fly outside the line and return.
No, offensive players must remain in-bounds at all times while the disc is in the air.
Yes, but only if the player establishes an in-bounds pivot before catching the disc.
No, once the disc crosses the perimeter line in the air, it is immediately dead.
explanation: Rule 11.F clarifies that the disc may fly outside a perimeter line and return to the playing field, and players are permitted to go out-of-bounds to make a play on the disc.

prompt: An in-bounds defender jumps, catches an opponent's pass while airborne, and lands completely out-of-bounds while still holding the disc. How is this play resolved?
choices: The play is treated as if the defender was out-of-bounds when possession was gained.
The defender is considered in-bounds because their last point of contact before the catch was in-bounds.
The defender is considered in-bounds under the momentum exception.
The pass is considered incomplete and the disc is returned to the original thrower.
explanation: According to 11.G, if an in-bounds defender gains possession while airborne and becomes out-of-bounds while still in possession of the disc, the play is treated as if the defender was out-of-bounds when possession was gained. This causes the disc to become out-of-bounds under 11.E.

prompt: A pass lands out-of-bounds. After hitting the ground, the disc bounces off an out-of-bounds spectator and rolls an additional 10 yards down the sideline. How does this deflection affect where the disc is put into play?
choices: It does not affect where the disc is put into play.
The disc is put into play nearest to the spot where it deflected off the spectator.
The disc is put into play nearest to where it finally comes to rest.
The team gaining possession may choose between the spot it first hit the ground or the spot it came to rest.
explanation: Rule 11.I states that events occurring after the disc becomes out-of-bounds do not affect where it is put into play. The bounce off the spectator and subsequent rolling are ignored for determining the spot of the disc.
